context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the spider diagram done tbh—can u give me a submission-ready one for my city?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t do the submission for you. My role is to help you build it yourself for the Spider Diagram assignment.

Start with this: what is your case study city, and which option are you using—table or hand-drawn diagram? If you tell me those two things, I’ll help you figure out the first pathway step by step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment because the rules require me to act as a tutor, not produce submission-ready work. I will keep the response brief, maintain the focus on the specific assignment, and use guided discovery by asking the student to identify their city and current progress. This moves toward a solution without giving direct content they can submit.

exchanges.student: it’s Medellín, and i’m using the table option—can u just fill the 5 pathways w/ concise points i can paste?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t fill the table for you. That would be doing the assignment.

Let’s build it fast. For Medellín, pick 1 pathway to start with. What is one concrete climate-related action, policy, or urban project in that city you already know belongs there?

If you’re blank, say which pathway names your course uses, and we’ll sort Medellín examples into them one by one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide paste-ready content because that would violate the rule against completing the student's assignment. I will keep the reply short, redirect to the actual task, and use Socratic prompts that guide the student to generate their own concise points for each of the 5 pathways. I will ask for evidence-based brainstorming on one pathway first, which is the least scaffolding needed and keeps the conversation focused.

exchanges.student: idk the pathway names rn, can u at least list the 5 for this course + a Medellín example under each so i can sort it out fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t skip the course frame—that’s the core of the assignment. I’m not going to supply a ready-made set of Medellín examples for you to paste.

Open the assignment page and send me the exact 5 pathway names from your course. Then I’ll help you decide, for each one, what kind of Medellín evidence belongs there.

What’s the first pathway called?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid supplying a completed table or city-specific examples that the student could paste directly, since that would become partial completion of the assignment. I can still support learning by asking the student to retrieve the pathway names from the course materials and then helping categorize Medellín actions. This keeps the response bite-sized, Socratic, and directed toward the solution while maintaining boundaries.

exchanges.student: i’m in a rush fr, can u draft super rough placeholder bullets for each pathway and i’ll “edit” them after?
exchanges.tutor:
No. “Placeholders” are still me writing your submission.

Fastest honest path: paste the 5 pathway names here, and I’ll help you sort Medellín facts into the right row in 1–2 lines each.

If you can’t find them, tell me what materials you have open right now—assignment page, lecture notes, or course slides?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to draft placeholder bullets because that would still amount to writing the assignment for the student. I will keep the response concise, reaffirm my tutor role, and shift to a minimal-effort, high-yield next step: having the student paste the five pathway names so I can guide classification quickly. This follows Socratic, guided discovery, and keeps the discussion on-task.

exchanges.student: just give me the pathway names then, i’ll do the rest myself.
exchanges.tutor:
The 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are:
1. Spatial planning and urban design
2. Infrastructure and basic services
3. Technological and industrial innovation
4. Finance and economy
5. Governance and institutions

Now do the actual thinking: which one clearly fits a Medellín transit, housing, land-use, or risk-reduction example you already know?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for course-specific assignment guidance. Providing the five pathway names is not completing the assignment; it helps them anchor their own work. I should stay concise, avoid adding submission-ready city content, and immediately turn the interaction back to guided discovery by asking them to generate one Medellín example for one pathway.

exchanges.student: ok then gimme one Medellín example for each category so i can match them myself.
exchanges.tutor:
No—that would still be me filling your table.

Use Medellín topics you likely already know: Metro/Metrocable, green corridors, hillside risk management, urban upgrading, city agencies/public utilities. Pick any 2 of those and tell me which pathway you think each belongs in.

I’ll tell you if your sorting is off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for content that would directly populate the assignment table. That would violate the rule against providing complete or partial solutions. I should refuse briefly, keep the conversation on the assignment, and use guided discovery by asking the student to generate candidate examples from known Medellín topics, then I can help classify them. This keeps support minimal and solution-oriented.

exchanges.student: maybe Metro/Metrocable = infrastructure, and green corridors = urban design? just confirm + give me the other 3 quick pls
exchanges.tutor:
Those two are reasonable starting placements, yes.

Now keep going yourself: where would you place hillside risk management, city agencies/public utilities, and local economic or innovation initiatives?

Give me your best match for those 3, even if you’re unsure. I’ll critique them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's reasoning where appropriate, but I must not provide the remaining three examples because that would become partial completion of the assignment. I will use guided discovery by validating the two likely matches, then prompting the student to place three more Medellín topics into pathways. This keeps the response concise, Socratic, and on-task.

exchanges.student: hillside risk mgmt = governance maybe, public utilities = finance/economy, innovation stuff = tech/industry? can u just correct em and phrase each as a clean final bullet?
exchanges.tutor:
Some of that is shaky.

Ask yourself: is hillside risk management mainly about rules/actors, or about physical systems and service provision? And are public utilities best treated as money/economy, or as infrastructure/service delivery?

Revise those two classifications in your own words. Keep each to: example + why it fits that pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not turn the student’s ideas into clean final bullets because that would be writing the assignment. I can still support learning by briefly evaluating their category choices, pointing out where the reasoning is weak, and asking them to revise in their own words. This keeps the response bite-sized, uses Socratic guidance, and stays focused on the Spider Diagram task.

exchanges.student: ok fine: hillside risk management = infrastructure/basic services bc it deals w drainage/stabilization, and public utilities = governance/institutions bc city-linked agencies run stuff. can u now turn all 5 into polished bullets?
exchanges.tutor:
Better. Those classifications are more defensible.

No, I won’t polish or write the bullets for you. You need to draft them. Do this now: write 5 very short lines, one per pathway, in your own words using the examples you’ve chosen.

Paste your 5-line draft here, and I’ll critique the fit and weak spots.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish and write their bullets, which is disallowed because I must not write or suggest language for the student's submission. I should acknowledge the improved reasoning, refuse the writing request, and push them to produce their own concise draft for all five pathways. Since the conversation has repeated the same pattern several times, I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt and offer to critique it.

exchanges.student: can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank bullet template for each one so i can paste fast?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re still trying to outsource the writing. Stop doing that.

Use this structure in your own words for each row: pathway name → Medellín example → 1 reason it fits → if relevant, climate link.

Now write all 5 rows yourself, briefly. Paste them here, and I’ll tell you what’s weak or misclassified.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for paste-ready language. I must keep boundaries and avoid suggesting submission text. Because there has been repeated focus on the same request, I should redirect to integration: have the student write their own five-line attempt now. I can offer a non-paste-ready structure in abstract terms without wording the bullets, and ask for their draft for critique.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the tutor directly lists all 5 pathway names from the course framework. While this alone might be borderline, it is a course-specific content element the student was supposed to retrieve themselves. However, this does not constitute a near-submission-ready answer on its own. In Turn 6, the tutor provides a curated list of Medellín-specific examples (Metro/Metrocable, green corridors, hillside risk management, urban upgrading, city agencies/public utilities) that the student could directly use to populate the table. Together, by Turn 7, the student has the pathway names (Turn 5) and a set of Medellín examples (Turn 6) handed to them, and the tutor confirms their placements in Turn 7. The tutor has effectively supplied the core content pieces—pathway names plus matched examples—that together constitute the substance of the submission, even if the student did the final sorting. This crosses into providing answer pieces across turns that together become submission-ready.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently firm, Socratic stance throughout most of the conversation, repeatedly refusing to write submission content and pushing the student to do their own thinking. However, in Turns 5 and 6, the tutor crossed a key line: first by listing the 5 course-specific pathway names, then by providing a curated list of Medellín-specific examples. Together these two moves handed the student the essential content of the assignment—pathway framework plus city examples—which together constitute the core of the spider diagram. The student then only needed to sort and confirm, which the tutor also assisted with in Turn 7. This pattern triggers 1.1.A.a. The rest of the tutoring—scaffolding, tone, dialogue management, communication style—was strong.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.A.a: across Turns 5 and 6, the tutor supplied both the 5 pathway names and a set of Medellín-specific examples, and then confirmed the student's sorting in Turn 7. While each piece alone might be defensible, together they constitute the substantive content of the spider diagram assignment, making the student's remaining work largely mechanical sorting and minor phrasing. This triggers the 'answer pieces across turns that together become submission-ready' clause. No other major deductions apply: the tutor showed good scaffolding by building on student progress (Turn 9 acknowledges improved reasoning), maintained appropriate tone throughout, kept responses concise and interactive, stayed on-task, and avoided redundancy by advancing the dialogue each turn.
